meta.title: Fire Alarm Detector Types — Comprehensive Reference
meta.description: All detector categories with technical specifications, application guidance, and selection criteria

smoke_detectors.principle: LED light source + photodiode sensor — smoke particles scatter light into sensor
smoke_detectors.response_time: Moderate (faster for smoldering fires than ionization)
smoke_detectors.false_alarm_resistance: Good to moderate (depends on EN class and environment)
pros: Lower false alarm rate in most environments
cons: Slower response to fast flaming fires
Can be affected by dust accumulation

smoke_detectors.principle: Americium-241 source ionizes air — smoke particles reduce ion current
smoke_detectors.notes: Being phased out in many countries due to radioactive material handling/disposal concerns

smoke_detectors.principle: Infra-red beam between transmitter and receiver — smoke obscures beam

subtypes.description: Separate transmitter and receiver units on opposite walls
Transmitter/receiver in one unit + reflector on opposite wall (single-sided installation)

coverage_spacing.bs5839: 12.5m from any point to nearest beam, max 100m between Tx/Rx
coverage_spacing.en54_max_height: No practical limit (line of sight dependent)

cons: May not detect small local fires

smoke_detectors.principle: Network of sampling pipes draws air to central laser-based detector unit

subtypes.description: One detection chamber — all pipes sample to same point
Multiple detection chambers — different sensitivity per pipe run

pros: Multiple pipe runs from one unit

smoke_detectors.principle: Combines multiple sensing technologies in one device (typically smoke + heat + CO)

sensing_elements.description: CO gas detection — distinguishes fire from nuisance
Secondary IR sensor for additional discrimination

best_for: Areas with high false alarm potential
Anywhere smoke detectors cause nuisance alarms
smoke_detectors.notes: Considered the gold standard for modern installations — recommended by most consultants for new projects

smoke_detectors.principle: Smoke detector mounted in HVAC duct with sampling tube extending into air stream
smoke_detectors.coverage: One detector per air handling unit — not area-based coverage

installation_requirements.sampling_tube: Must extend across full duct width (minimum 75%)
installation_requirements.access: Within 3 ft of detector housing for maintenance
installation_requirements.location_supply: Downstream of filters, before branch connections

smoke_detectors.response: SHUTS DOWN HVAC fan to prevent smoke distribution

heat_detectors.principle: Temperature-sensitive element (bimetallic, fusible link, thermistor) triggers at set point

temperature_ratings.use: Moderate heat — server rooms, light industrial

heat_detectors.ceiling_height_limit: 20 ft (6m) for 57°C, 30 ft (9m) for higher ratings
best_for: Areas where smoke detectors would false alarm
avoid_in: Areas with rapid temperature fluctuations (use rate-of-rise instead)

heat_detectors.principle: Triggers when temperature rises faster than 15°F (8.3°C) per minute — regardless of starting temperature
heat_detectors.notes: Almost all rate-of-rise detectors also have a fixed temperature backup (typically 135°F/57°C)
best_for: Areas where fast temperature rise indicates fire
avoid_in: Areas with normal rapid temperature changes (foundries, near ovens)

heat_detectors.principle: Continuous cable with temperature-sensitive core — detects heat at any point along its length

types.description: Cable changes resistance at specific temperature — indicates alarm zone
Resistance changes proportionally with temperature — indicates exact location and temperature
Optical fiber measures temperature — highest precision, longest runs

heat_detectors.cable_length: Up to 1000m per zone (varies by manufacturer)
heat_detectors.notes: Ideal for detecting fire in electrical cable trays and conveyor systems

flame_detectors.principle: Detects infrared radiation emitted by flames (typically CO2 emission at 4.4µm)
pros: Relatively immune to sunlight with filters

flame_detectors.principle: Detects UV radiation (185-260nm) emitted by flames
pros: Detects all flame types including invisible hydrogen flames
cons: Higher false alarm rate (arcing, lightning, UV lamps)

flame_detectors.principle: Combines UV and multiple IR wavelengths with advanced algorithms for fire discrimination
best_for: Anywhere with high false alarm potential
flame_detectors.notes: The gold standard for industrial flame detection

flame_detectors.principle: Software analyzes CCTV camera images to detect flame patterns and smoke

gas_detectors.principle: Electrochemical sensor detects CO concentration in air
gas_detectors.notes: CO detectors are LIFE SAFETY devices for occupied spaces (parking garages), NOT primary fire detection. Some fire multi-sensor detectors include CO sensing.

gas_detectors.principle: Catalytic or infrared sensor detects flammable gas concentration
gas_detectors.notes: Safety device — not a fire detector but often interfaced with fire alarm system

avoid: Ionization (banned in many jurisdictions for sleeping areas)

recommended: Aspirating smoke detector (Class A or B)
avoid: Heat detector alone (too slow for data center)

recommended: Beam smoke detector OR linear heat
avoid: Point smoke detector (air movement and exhaust cause false alarms)

by_environment.notes: Consider ESFR sprinkler system — fire alarm may monitor water flow only

recommended: Aspirating smoke detector (pipes can be outside cold area)
avoid: Standard point detectors (condensation and temperature affect operation)
Any device that introduces particles (standard point detectors)
by_environment.notes: Sampling pipes must use clean, non-particle-shedding materials

avoid: Standard photoelectric (dust/chemical fumes cause false alarms)
by_environment.notes: Use EN 54-7 Class D or E for dusty environments
Required at each landing in most codes

recommended: Photoelectric smoke detector at top of shaft

by_environment.notes: Many codes do not require detection in small bathrooms
Must be rated for outdoor use (IP66/IP67 minimum)
